Sampling and Sample Preparation
Chapter 28
Quantitative Chemical Analysis , Daniel C. Harris and Charles A. Lucy, © 2020 W. H. Freeman and Company

Chapter Outline
Section 28-1 Statistics of Sampling
Section 28-2 Dissolving Samples for Analysis
Section 28-3 Sample Preparation Techniques

Cocaine Use? Ask the River
After ingestion, cocaine is converted to bezoylecgonine and excreted in urine.
Collect representative composite samples of water from Po River and samples of wastewater entering treatment plants
Preconcentrate minute quantities from large quantities of water via solid-phase extraction
Wash, separate, and analyze samples
Cocaine use was estimated as 27  5 100-mg does of cocaine per 1 000 people per day.
Cocaine use is higher than people admit in a survey.

Sampling and Sample Preparation (1 of 2)
A chemical analysis is meaningless unless you begin with a meaningful sample.
A strategy for selecting a representative sample is necessary.
Sampling: the process of collecting a representative sample for analysis
Heterogeneous: different composition from place to place in a material
Homogeneous: same composition throughout the material
Sample preparation : the series of steps that convert a representative bulk sample into a form suitable for analysis
Usually required with real-world samples
Remove substances that interfere with analysis
Convert analyte into form suitable for analysis

Sampling
Consider the concentration of nitrate in sediment beneath a lake.
Depth profile of nitrate in sediment from freshwater Lake Søbygård in Denmark
Nitrate drops by two orders of magnitude in the first 3 mm below the surface.
It makes an enormous difference whether you select a core sample that is 1 m deep or skim the top 2 mm of sediment for analysis.
Figure 28-1

Sampling and Sample Preparation (2 of 2)
Bulk Sample (or Gross Sample)
Taken from the lot for analysis or for archiving for future reference
Must be representative of the lot
Critical for producing a valid analysis
Figure 28-2
Lot: total material from which samples are taken
Laboratory Sample
Must have same composition as bulk
Uses aliquots for individual analysis

Sample Storage
Properly storing a sample requires care.
Composition may change with time after collection because of chemical changes, reaction with air, or interaction of sample with container.
Glass containers may act as an ion exchanger, altering the concentrations of trace ions and proteins in solution.
Plastic containers must be washed before use. Polyethylene containers can contribute a measurable flux of analyte even after being soaked in acid for several months.
Steel needles are an avoidable source of metal contamination in biochemical analysis.
Table 28-1 Manganese concentration of serum stored in washed and unwashed polyethylene containers

Sampling and Storage
Your lab notebook should describe how a sample was:
Collected
Stored
Handled
Analyzed
“Unless the complete history of any sample is known with certainty, the analyst is well advised not to spend his [or her] time in analyzing it.”
—R. E. Thiers

Statistics of Sampling (1 of 3)
For random errors, the overall variance is the sum of the variance of the analytical procedure and the variance of the sampling operation.
If either s a or s s is sufficiently smaller than the other, there is little point in trying to reduce the smaller one.

Statistics of Sampling (2 of 3)
Consider a random mixture of two kinds of solid particles.
If n particles (A and B) are drawn at random.
The expected number of particles of type A are np and the standard deviation of many drawings follows the binomial distribution.

Example: Statistics of Drawing Particles (1 of 4)
A mixture contains 1% KCl particles and 99% KNO 3 particles. If 10 4 particles are taken, what is the expected number of KCl particles, and what will be the standard deviation if the experiment is repeated many times?

Example: Statistics of Drawing Particles (2 of 4)
Solution: The expected number is
Expected number of KCl particles = np = (10 4 ) ( 0.01) = 100 particles
and the standard deviation will be

Example: Statistics of Drawing Particles (3 of 4)
Solution: The standard deviation
applies to both kinds of particles . The standard deviation is 9.9% of the expected number of KCl particles, but only 0.1% of the expected number of KNO 3 particles ( nq = 9 900).

Choosing a Sample Size
Sampling of 24 Na in Human Liver
Once homogenized, the sample is a suspension of small particles in water.
How much of a random, heterogeneous mixture should be analyzed to reduce the sampling variance?
K s is the mass of sample that will produce a relative sampling standard deviation of 1%.
Figure 28-3

Calculation of Sampling Constant
Figure 28-3
Sampling constant: mR 2 = K s

Sampling Constant, K s
Data for Mn in Powdered Algae
K s is approximately proportional to sample mass ( m ) over six orders of magnitude.

Choosing the Number of Replicate Analyses
If we assume the analytical uncertainty ( s a ) is negligible, then t he sampling contribution to the overall uncertainty can be reduced by analyzing more samples .
If a single sample is expected to give s s = 7%, how many 0.7-g samples must be analyzed to give 95% confidence that the mean is known to within  4%?
Required number of replicate analyses:
e is the uncertainty. Student’s t is from Table 4-4.
Both s s and e must be expressed as absolute uncertainties or both as relative uncertainties.
Note: Need prior knowledge of standard deviation .

Example: Sampling a Random Bulk Material (1 of 5)
How many 0.7-g samples must be analyzed to give 95% confidence that the mean is known to within ±4%?

Example: Sampling a Random Bulk Material (2 of 5)
Solution: A 0.7-g sample gives s s = 7%, and we are seeking e = 4%. We will express both uncertainties in relative form. Taking t = 1.960 (from Table 4-4 for 95% confidence and ∞ degrees of freedom) as a starting value, we find

Example: Sampling a Random Bulk Material (3 of 5)
Solution: For n = 12, there are 11 degrees of freedom, so a second trial value of Student’s t (interpolated from Table 4-4) is 2.209. A second cycle of calculation gives
For n = 15, there are 14 degrees of freedom and t = 2.150, which gives

Example: Sampling a Random Bulk Material (4 of 5)
Solution: For n = 14, there are 13 degrees of freedom and t = 2.170, which gives

Sample Preparation
• Sample preparation is the series of steps required to transform a sample so that it’s suitable for analysis .
Goals of Sample Preparation/Sample Treatment
• Sample not present in a matrix suitable for the chosen method
 extracting analyte from a complex matrix
 dissolving the sample – grinding, acid digestion or fusion, combustion (dry ashing ), wet ashing

• Eliminate substances in the sample that interfere with the measurement of the analyte .
* removing or masking interfering species
• Concentrating a dilute analyte to a level that can be measured.
place analyte at level that’s suitable for the detection.
Chemically converting analyte into a detectable form.
*place the analyte into a chemical form that can be studied by your method of choice.

Dissolving Samples for Analysis
Once a bulk sample is selected, a laboratory sample must be prepared for analysis.
Laboratory samples are usually dissolved for analysis.
It is important to dissolve the entire sample to ensure all analyte is dissolved.
If mild dissolution conditions are insufficient, acid digestion or fusion may be used.
Organic material may be destroyed by combustion ( dry ashing ) or oxidation with liquid reagents ( wet ashing ). This leaves inorganic elements in a suitable form for analysis.

Grinding
Solids are ground in a mortar and pestle .
Agate mortar (or similar porcelain, mullite, or alumina) is designed for grinding small particles into fine powder.
Less expensive mortars tend to be more porous and easily scratched.
Ceramic can be cleaned by wiping with wet tissue and washing with distilled H 2 O.
Difficult residues can be removed by grinding with 4 M HCl.
Boron carbide is five times harder than agate and less likely to contaminate sample.
Figure 28-5

Ball Mill and Shatterbox Mill
Ball mill uses steel or ceramic balls rotated inside a drum to crush sample to fine powder.
Shatterbox laboratory mill spins a puck and ring inside a grinding container, pulverizing up to 100 g of material.
Figure 28-6

Dissolving Inorganic Materials with Acid
An array of acids are commonly used for dissolving inorganic materials.
Substances that do not dissolve in nonoxidizing acids may dissolve in the oxidizing acids HNO 3 ; hot, concentrated H 2 SO 4 ; or hot, concentrated HClO 4 .
Aqua regia is a 3:1 (vol/vol) mixture of HCl:NHO 3 used to dissolve Au and Pt.
Strong oxidants can dissolve difficult materials.
Dissolving with acids can be DANGEROUS .
It is important to understand all hazards before attempting
and to follow all safety protocols.
See Table 28.5: acids for sample dissolution

Microwave Digestion
A Teflon-lined bomb (sealed vessel) heated in a microwave oven.
Complex samples can be digested.
Teflon is required because metal would absorb microwaves.
Bomb should be cooled prior to opening to prevent loss of volatile products.
Figure 28-7

Dissolving Inorganic Materials by Fusion
Substances that will not dissolve in acid can usually be dissolved in hot, molten inorganic flux .
Finely powdered unknowns are mixed with flux.
Fusion (melting) is carried out in a Pt-Au alloy crucible at 300°–1 200°C.
Disadvantage: Impurities are introduced by the large amount of solid reagent.
Figure 28-8

Decomposition of Organic Substances
Digestion of organic material can be classified as
Dry ashing : no liquid used
Halogens in coal
Metals in plant material
Wet ashing : liquid used
Metals in animal tissue
Fuming HNO 3 , H 2 SO 4 , or H 2 SO 4 and HNO 3 can be added to organic substance in glass digestion tube
Figure 28-9

Digestion with a High-Pressure Asher
Figure 28-10
The high-pressure asher uses a resistive heating element inside a sealed chamber for digestion at temperatures up to 270°C under a pressure up to 140 bar.
High pressure allows acids to be heated to high temperature without boiling.
At high temperature, HNO 3 oxidizes organic matter without assistance from H 2 SO 4 .
Hydrogen peroxide reduces nitrogen oxides generated by  digestion of organic matter.

Wet Ashing with Refluxing HNO 3 -HClO 4
Figure 28-11
Widely applicable, but hazardous, procedure.
Perchloric acid has caused numerous explosions.
First, heat the sample slowly to boiling with HNO 3 but without HClO 4 .
Boil to near dryness to destroy easily oxidized material.
Add fresh HNO 3 and repeat several times.
Cool the sample and add HClO 4 and heat again.
HNO 3 should be present during the HClO 4 treatment.
